original_abstract:
Controlling the surface strain of nanoparticles (NPs) is a sophisticated method for modulating surface chemistry and enhancing catalytic performance. This study demonstrates that surface Pt strain in core/shell FePt/Pt NPs—featuring approximately three atomic layers of Pt—can be systematically tuned by inducing a structural transition in the core from a disordered cubic solid solution (fcc) to a chemically ordered tetragonal intermetallic (fct) phase. Electrochemical characterization reveals that the fct-FePt/Pt NPs exhibit significantly higher oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) activity compared to their fcc counterparts. Quantum mechanics–molecular mechanics (QM–MM) simulations suggest that this enhancement stems from the fct core's ability to relieve overcompressed strain within the Pt shell, bringing the oxygen binding energy closer to the theoretical optimum.

Furthermore, this strain-tuning effect was optimized by partially substituting Fe with Cu to form trimetallic fct-FeCuPt/Pt NPs. The addition of Cu further calibrates the surface strain, resulting in a catalyst that is nearly ten times more active in terms of specific activity than commercial Pt catalysts. These findings highlight structure-induced surface strain control as a powerful strategy for designing next-generation catalysts for the ORR and other important chemical transformations.
humanized_abstract:
Control of the surface strain of nanoparticles (NPs) via adjusting the surface chemistry for enhancing catalytic activity is considered as a highly sophisticated strategy. In this work, the surface Pt strain in a core/shell structure of FePt/Pt nanoparticles was systematically controlled via transforming the core from a disordered cubic solid solution (fcc) to a chemically ordered tetragonal intermetallic (fct) structure while keeping the thickness of the Pt shell at around 3 atomic layers, and thereby significantly enhancing the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) activity of the resultant fct-FePt/Pt NPs over that of the fcc-FePt/Pt NPs. Via quantum mechanics–molecular mechanics (QM–MM) simulation method, it has been confirmed that higher ORR activity of fct-FePt/Pt than fcc-FePt/Pt could be ascribed to the structural transformation in the core that benefited in relieving overcompressed strain in Pt shell of the core/shell structure, and consequently in approaching to the optimum energy for oxygen binding on the NP surface.

The strain-tuning effect was further optimized by introducing a partial Cu substitution into the Fe site of the trimetallic fct-FeCuPt/Pt NPs. The Cu substitution serves to fine-tune the surface strain of the catalyst, yielding a catalyst material with a specific activity that is approximately ten times higher than that of commercial Pt catalysts. Structure-induced surface strain control was demonstrated to be an effective design principle for catalysts toward the ORR and other key reactions.
